summary: Small CI/tooling PR that changed objdiff change reporting from a hard build failure to a warning. The GitHub workflow now always exits successfully after running `tools/diff_changes.py`, and the script's broken-function message was changed from a GitHub Actions error annotation to a warning-style message.

what_changed.details: After generating an objdiff changes report and running `python tools/diff_changes.py "$changes"`, the workflow no longer captures and exits with the script status. It now runs `exit 0`, so detected broken functions do not fail the CI job.

When `process_input` finds broken functions, the printed GitHub annotation was downgraded from `::error::One or more functions were broken!` to `::warning One or more functions were broken!` while still dumping the JSON result and exiting with status 1.

smart_moves: Separated local/tool semantics from CI policy: `diff_changes.py` still exits nonzero when broken functions are found, preserving its usefulness as a strict checker outside this workflow, while the GitHub workflow explicitly decides not to fail the build.
Kept objdiff change visibility in CI by continuing to run the report and print the JSON details, but made the result advisory instead of blocking.
decomp_lessons: For decompilation progress workflows, regressions or broken-function reports can be made visible without blocking unrelated work by downgrading CI annotations and overriding the workflow exit status.
If a tool is useful both as a strict local checker and as CI reporting, prefer changing the CI wrapper behavior rather than weakening the tool's exit code globally.
Small CI policy changes should keep diagnostic output intact so reviewers still see affected functions even when the job is allowed to pass.
naming_conventions: `diff_changes.py` names its main input handler `process_input(input_file: Optional[str])`.
The workflow uses descriptive shell variables such as `changes`, `expected`, and `current` around `objdiff-cli report changes`.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs touching `tools/diff_changes.py` to see whether the warning annotation syntax was adjusted.
Search for CI workflow changes involving `objdiff-cli report changes` or broken-function reporting.
Check whether downstream tooling expects `diff_changes.py` to preserve its nonzero exit code outside GitHub Actions.
